Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.

Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

Level 1: you have used 80 actions; a strong score needs about 39 or fewer. You are 2.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The DOWN actions had `no_impact: True`, which means the blue block didn't move. Looking at the image, the blue block is now near the bottom, just above the U-shapes. So the blue block did move DOWN, but the `no_impact` check says it didn't change gameplay.
Wait, that's inconsistent. Let me check the current blue block position.
Actually, looking at the image, the blue block is now near the bottom, just above the U-shapes. So the blue block did move DOWN. But the result says `no_impact: True`. This is confusing.
Let me check the current blue block position to understand what happened.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current blue block position
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        print(f"Blue block: boundary={n['boundary']}")
        break
# Check all objects
for n in seg:
    print(f"ID {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
